statement: The Stjórnlagaráð (Constitutional Council) was a 25-member body elected by the Icelandic public on 27 November 2010 to draft a new constitution. It worked from April to July 2011 and produced a comprehensive draft with 114 articles. Key provisions included: (1) a natural resources clause (auðlindaákvæði) declaring national ownership of natural resources not in private hands; (2) an information rights clause (upplýsingaréttur) establishing public right of access to government documents; (3) provisions for increased personal votes (persónukjör) in parliamentary elections, allowing voters to influence which candidates on party lists win seats; (4) a 'one person, one vote' principle (jafnt vægi atkvæða) to equalise the weight of votes across constituencies, addressing the long-standing disparity where rural votes carried significantly more weight than urban votes; (5) provisions for national referendums initiated by citizens (þjóðarfrumkvæði); (6) environmental protection as a constitutional right. Former President Vigdís Finnbogadóttir (in office 1980–1996) described the process as one of the most democratic constitutional exercises known, noting the combination of a nationally elected drafting body, public crowd-sourcing of ideas, and transparent deliberation.
caveats: The Constitutional Council's election had very low turnout (~37%) and was declared unconstitutional by the Supreme Court on procedural grounds (ballot secrecy violations), though Alþingi subsequently appointed the same 25 individuals as a parliamentary committee. The draft was never enacted despite the 2012 advisory referendum. The personukjör provisions were less detailed than some accounts suggest — the draft established the principle but left implementation details to ordinary legislation.

related_entries.statement: On 20 October 2012, Iceland held a six-question advisory referendum on constitutional reform proposals drafted by the Stjórnlagaráð (Constitutional Council). The first and most significant question — 'Do you wish the proposals of the Constitutional Council to form the basis of a new Constitution?' — received 66.9% yes votes (49,486 yes vs 24,521 no). All six questions received majority yes votes. Turnout was 48.9% (115,890 of approximately 237,000 eligible voters), significantly lower than typical parliamentary election turnout of 80–85%. The referendum was advisory (ráðgefandi), not legally binding, as the Icelandic constitution does not provide for binding referendums on policy questions. Despite the clear majority in favour, no subsequent Alþingi has enacted the draft into law.

Following the 2012 advisory referendum in which 66.9% supported using the Constitutional Council's draft as the basis for a new constitution, successive Alþingi sessions have failed to enact constitutional reform. The centre-right government of Sigmundur Davíð Gunnlaugsson and Bjarni Benediktsson (2013–2016) effectively shelved the draft, arguing it needed further expert review. Subsequent governments (2016–2017, 2017–2021, 2021–2024) introduced partial constitutional bills — notably on natural resources and electoral reform — but none passed into law. The Stjórnarskrárfélagið (Constitutional Society) and other civil society organisations have campaigned to honour the referendum result, framing parliamentary inaction as a democratic deficit. As of early 2026, no provisions of the 2011 draft have been enacted, making it one of the most protracted constitutional reform efforts in modern European democracies.

The Constitutional Council's 2011 draft constitution includes a natural resources clause (auðlindaákvæðið) stating: 'Iceland's natural resources which are not in private ownership are the common and perpetual property of the nation. No one may acquire the natural resources or their attached rights for ownership or permanent use, and they may never be sold or mortgaged.' The clause envisions a licensing regime with leases limited to 'a modest period of time.' This provision was the subject of Question 2 in the 2012 referendum ('Do you wish that a new Constitution include a provision on national ownership of natural resources, as is in the draft?'), which received 82.9% yes — the highest approval of any of the six questions. The provision is considered economically significant as Iceland's key export sectors (fishing, energy-intensive industry, tourism) rely on natural resources.
